This documentation provides a comprehensive guide on how to use the `ThemeContext`, including installation, basic usage, customization, dynamic updates, and a complete example.
This should help users quickly understand and implement theme management in their applications.

## Introduction

The `ThemeContext` provides a way to manage and share theme configurations across your application.
It allows you to easily switch between different themes and ensure a consistent look and feel throughout your UI.

The `ThemeContext `provides a flexible and efficient way to manage themes in your application.
By using the `ThemeProvider` and `useContext` hook, you can easily apply and switch themes, ensuring a consistent and visually appealing user experience.

## Installation

Before using `ThemeContext`, ensure you have installed the necessary dependencies.
Typically, this context is part of a larger UI library, such as `@flexnative/theme-context`.

```bash
npm install @flexnative/theme-context
# or
yarn add @flexnative/theme-context
```

## API

```typescript showLineNumbers
import { ThemeContext } from '@flexnative/theme-context';
```

## Component
**ThemeContext**

Type: 
```typescript showLineNumbers
const ThemeContext: React.Context<ThemeContextProps<any>> =
  React.createContext<ThemeContextProps<any>>({
    state: defaultTheme()
  });
```

## Properties

|  Name   | Type | Required | Default Value | Description |
| -- | -- | -- | -- | -- |
| `state` | `BaseTheme<TColors>` | **true** | `defaultTheme()` | The current theme state, which includes colors, font sizes, borders, and other theme-related properties. |
| `setColorScheme` | `(scheme: ColorSchemeName) => void` | **false** | `undefined` | Function to set the color scheme (light or dark). This allows dynamic switching between themes based on user preference or system settings. |
| `onChangeTheme` | `(theme: BaseTheme<TColors>) => void` | **false** | `undefined` | Callback function that is called when the theme changes. This can be used to trigger side effects or update other parts of the application when the theme is switched. |
| `theme` | `BaseTheme<TColors>` | **true** | `defaultTheme()` | The current theme object that contains all the theme properties. This is the main object you will use to access theme values in your components. |


## Usage

### 1. Importing the Context
First, import the `ThemeContext` and `ThemeProvider` from the `@flexnative/theme-context` package:

```typescript showLineNumbers
import { ThemeContext, ThemeProvider } from '@flexnative/theme-context';
```

### 2. Providing the Theme
To enable the theme context for your components, you must wrap your application's root component (or a relevant part of your component tree) with the `ThemeProvider`.
This provider will make the theme available to all child components that consume the context.

:::danger CAUTION
Any attempt to use the theme hooks outside of a `ThemeProvider` will result in an error.
:::

```typescript showLineNumbers
import React from 'react';
// highlight-next-line
import { ThemeProvider } from '@flexnative/theme-context';

const App = () => {
  const theme = {
    palette: {
      primary: {
        main: '#3f51b5',
      },
      secondary: {
        main: '#f50057',
      },
    },
    // ... other theme configurations
  };

  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      {/* Your application content here */}
      <Content />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
};

export default App;
```

### 3. Consuming the Theme
You can consume the theme in any functional component using the `useContext` hook:

```typescript showLineNumbers
import React, { useContext } from 'react';
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
// highlight-next-line
import { ThemeContext } from '@flexnative/theme-context';

const Content = () => {
  const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);

  return (
    <View style={{ backgroundColor: theme.palette.primary.main, color: 'white' }}>
      <Text>Themed Content</Text>
      <Text>This content is styled using the theme from ThemeContext.</Text>
    </View>
  );
};

export default Content;
```

Alternatively, you can use the `ThemeContext.Consumer` component in class components:

```typescript showLineNumbers
import React, { Component } from 'react';
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
// highlight-next-line
import { ThemeContext } from '@flexnative/theme-context';

class Content extends Component {
  render() {
    return (
      <ThemeContext.Consumer>
        {theme => (
          <View style={{ backgroundColor: theme.palette.secondary.main, color: 'white' }}>
            <Text>Themed Content</Text>
            <Text>This content is styled using the theme from ThemeContext.</Text>
          </View>
        )}
      </ThemeContext.Consumer>
    );
  }
}

export default Content;
```

### 4. Customizing the Theme
You can customize the theme by providing a different theme object to the `ThemeProvider`.
This allows you to easily switch between different themes or modify the existing theme based on user preferences.

```typescript showLineNumbers
import React from 'react';
// highlight-next-line
import { ThemeProvider } from '@flexnative/theme-context';

const DarkTheme = {
  palette: {
    primary: {
      main: '#212121',
    },
    secondary: {
      main: '#9e9e9e',
    },
  },
  // ... other dark theme configurations
};

const App = () => {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={DarkTheme}>
      {/* Your application content here */}
      <Content />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
};

export default App;
```

For more details on how to customize the theme, refer to the [Customizing Theme](./customizing-theme) documentation.

### 5. Updating the Theme Dynamically

To update the theme dynamically, you can use a state variable to hold the current theme and update it using useState.
Pass this state variable to the `ThemeProvider`.

```typescript showLineNumbers
import React, { useState } from 'react';
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
import Button from '@flexnative/buttons';
// highlight-next-line
import { ThemeProvider } from '@flexnative/theme-context';

const LightTheme = {
  palette: {
    primary: {
      main: '#3f51b5',
    },
    secondary: {
      main: '#f50057',
    },
  },
  // ... other light theme configurations
};

const DarkTheme = {
  palette: {
    primary: {
      main: '#212121',
    },
    secondary: {
      main: '#9e9e9e',
    },
  },
  // ... other dark theme configurations
};

const App = () => {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useState(LightTheme);

  const toggleTheme = () => {
    setTheme(theme === LightTheme ? DarkTheme : LightTheme);
  };

  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      <Button onClick={toggleTheme}>Toggle Theme</Button>
      {/* Your application content here */}
      <Content />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
};

export default App;
```

## Available Hooks

We provide several custom hooks to interact with the theme context. Using these hooks is the recommended way to access theme data and functions.

### useThemeContext

This is the primary hook for interacting with the theme. It gives you access to the complete theme state object and the functions to update it.

**Returns:** An object containing:
- `state`: The current theme object (`{ name, colors, fontSize, ... }`).
- `setTheme`: A function to switch the entire theme.
- `setColors`: A function to update the theme's colors.
- `setColorScheme`: A function to change the color scheme (e.g., 'light' or 'dark').

**Example:**

```typescript showLineNumbers
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
import Button from '@flexnative/buttons';
// highlight-next-line
import { useThemeContext } from './ThemeContext';

// Define your custom color types for full type-safety (optional)
type MyColors = {
  specialAccent: string;
};

const ThemeControlPanel = () => {
  // Use the generic to get type safety on your custom colors
  const { state, setTheme } = useThemeContext<MyColors>();

  return (
    <View>
      <Text>Current theme: {state.name}</Text>
      <Text>Primary color: {state.colors.primary}</Text>
      {/* Your custom color is fully typed */}
      <Text>Special Accent: {state.colors.specialAccent}</Text>
      <Button onClick={() => setTheme('dark')}>Switch to Dark Theme</Button>
    </View>
  );
};
```

### useThemeState

A convenience hook that returns only the theme's `state` object. Use this when you only need to **read** theme values (like spacing, fonts, or colors) and don't need the updater functions.

**Returns:** The theme state object (`{ name, colors, fontSize, ... }`).

**Example:**

```tsx
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
import { useThemeState } from './ThemeContext';

const Header = () => {
  const themeState = useThemeState();

  return (
    <View style={{ padding: themeState.spacing.medium }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: themeState.fontSize.large, color: themeState.colors.text }}>
        Welcome
      </Text>
    </View>
  );
};
```

### useThemeColors

A specialized convenience hook that returns only the theme's `colors` object. Use this when your component's styling is only dependent on the theme colors.

**Returns:** The theme colors object (`{ primary, secondary, text, ... }`).

**Example:**

```typescript showLineNumbers
import Button from '@flexnative/buttons';
import { useThemeColors } from './ThemeContext';

const ThemedButton = ({ children }) => {
  const colors = useThemeColors();

  return (
    <Button style={{ backgroundColor: colors.primary, color: colors.white }}>
      {children}
    </Button>
  );
};
```
## Advanced: Typing Custom Colors

All hooks are generic and can accept a type parameter (`<TColors>`) that defines your application's custom colors. This provides full type-safety and autocompletion for your custom theme extensions, preventing runtime errors and improving the developer experience.

**Steps:**

1.  **Define your custom color type.** This type should extend the `BaseColors` to include your new color definitions.
2.  **Pass this type to the hook** when you use it in a component.

**Example:**

```typescript showLineNumbers
// 1. Define your custom colors in a types file
export type AppSpecificColors = {
  ctaBackground: string;
  subtleBorder: string;
};

// 2. Use it in your component
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
import { useThemeColors } from './ThemeContext';
import { AppSpecificColors } from './path/to/types';

const CallToAction = () => {
  // Pass your custom type to the hook
  const colors = useThemeColors<AppSpecificColors>();

  return (
    <View style={{ backgroundColor: colors.ctaBackground, borderColor: colors.subtleBorder }}>
      <Text>Click Me!</Text>
    </View>
  );
};
```

## Legacy: Direct Context Consumption

While hooks are the recommended approach, it's also possible to use the `ThemeContext.Consumer` component. This can be useful in class components or other scenarios where hooks are not available.

```typescript showLineNumbers
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
import ThemeContext from './ThemeContext';

const MyComponent = () => (
  <ThemeContext.Consumer>
    {({ state }) => (
      <View>
        <Text>Current Theme Name: {state.name}</Text>
        <Text style={{ color: state.colors.primary }}>This text is in the primary color.</Text>
      </View>
    )}
  </ThemeContext.Consumer>
);
```

## Example

Here is a complete example that demonstrates how to use the ThemeContext to switch between light and dark themes:

```typescript showLineNumbers
import React, { useState, useContext } from 'react';
import { View, Text } from 'react-native';
import Button from '@flexnative/buttons';
import { ThemeProvider, ThemeContext } from '@flexnative/theme-context';

const LightTheme = {
  palette: {
    primary: {
      main: '#3f51b5',
    },
    secondary: {
      main: '#f50057',
    },
    background: {
      default: '#fafafa',
    },
  },
};

const DarkTheme = {
  palette: {
    primary: {
      main: '#212121',
    },
    secondary: {
      main: '#9e9e9e',
    },
    background: {
      default: '#303030',
    },
  },
};

const ThemeSwitcher = () => {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useState(LightTheme);

  const toggleTheme = () => {
    setTheme(theme === LightTheme ? DarkTheme : LightTheme);
  };

  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      <View style={{ padding: '20px', backgroundColor: theme.palette.background.default, color: theme.palette.primary.main }}>
        <Text>Themed Application</Text>
        <Button onClick={toggleTheme}>Toggle Theme</Button>
        <Content />
      </View>
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
};

const Content = () => {
  const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);

  return (
    <div style={{ marginTop: '20px' }}>
      <p>Current Theme: {theme === LightTheme ? 'Light' : 'Dark'}</p>
      <p>This is themed content.</p>
    </div>
  );
};

export default ThemeSwitcher;
```
